Three flocks of sheep. Priests and Levites and Israelites.
2 Now the first’ inhabitants that dwelt in their possessions in ‘their cities were, the Israelites, the priests, Levites, and the Nethinim.

It is possible that the Nethinim, mentioned with the priests and the Levites, were the deacons of ancient times. (See 1 Chronicles 9:2; Ezra 2:43; 7:7, 24; Nehemiah 10:28; 11:3.) We read of them in Ezra 8:20: "Also of the Nethinims, whom David and the princes (apostles?) had appointed [ordained] for the service of the Levites, two hundred and twenty Nethinims: all of them were expressed by name." (John A. Tvedtnes, The Church of the Old Testament, p.44)
Three flocks of sheep. Priests and Levites and Israelites.

If Lehi was a descendant of Manasseh, shouldn’t he have lived in the northern “kingdom of Israel” which was sacked in 721 BC? This is an excellent question. If Lehi’s forefathers had lived with the rest of the tribe of Manasseh, in the northern kingdom, they would have been sacked by the Assyrians (see maps 5 & 9). They would have become part of the “lost ten tribes.” Apparently, many of the “children” of Manasseh and Ephraim dwelt at Jerusalem with the members of the southern kingdom, composed…
and the Nethinim, and the children of Solomon’s servants. 4 And at Jerusalem dwelt ‘certain

68:13-24 Firstborn Among the Sons of Aaron May Serve As the Presiding Bishop
There was a man in the land of Uz This means in the land of Aram, as it is written (Gen. 22: 21): “Uz, his firstborn,” of the sons of Nahor.

FOR THERE WAS NOT A HOUSE WHERE THERE WAS NOT ONE DEAD. Rashi comments: “If there was a firstborn there, he died; if there was no firstborn there, the chief person in the house died because he is called b’chor (firstborn), as it is said, I also will appoint him firstborn.275Psalms 89:27. Another interpretation: The Egyptian women led dissolute lives under their husbands and bore children from unmarried young men. Thus they had many firstborn sons, sometimes [as many as] five to one woman, each o

לארכה, according to its length, from east to west; ולרחבה, and to its width from north to south.
